title: EXCLUSIVE: UAE clinic says thousands join waitlist for Eli Lilly’s weight-loss pill after early approval -
summary: Ali Hashemi, Co-Founder of Metabolic, said demand reflects both patient interest in needle-free treatment options and the scale of obesity-related disease across the region
thbAction: Map the news to THB's provider CRM for referral growth, patient lifecycle engagement, and multi-facility relationship management across hospital and provider networks.
contentHtml:
Metabolic, a UAE-based specialist medicine provider, said the response since the country approved Foundayo (orforglipron) has been immediate, with a growing waiting list ahead of broader patient access.

The medicine is a once-daily GLP-1 pill designed for adults with obesity, or those who are overweight with weight-related health conditions.

The UAE became the second country globally to approve the treatment after the US Food and Drug Administration cleared it on April 1.

The Emirates Drug Establishment approved the medicine two days later, accelerating its launch in a market increasingly seen as an early adopter of advanced therapies.

Ali Hashemi, Co-Founder and CEO of Metabolic, said demand reflects both patient interest in needle-free treatment options and the scale of obesity-related disease across the region.

“There are already several thousand patients on the waiting list,” Hashemi told Arabian Business, adding that the clinic does not view the drug as a cosmetic product but as a medical treatment for chronic metabolic disease.

He said patients would not receive prescriptions automatically and would instead go through a full clinical screening process that includes physician consultations, medical history reviews, blood testing and cardiometabolic risk assessments.

Eligibility criteria mirror those used for injectable GLP-1 medicines: a body mass index above 30, or above 27 where obesity-related co-morbidities are present.

“We look at BMI, waist circumference, diabetes or prediabetes status, blood pressure, lipids, liver health, medication history and prior weight-loss attempts,” Hashemi said.

“But we also assess whether the patient is ready to engage in a structured programme.”

That programme includes nutrition planning, strength training, sleep support, metabolic monitoring and long-term maintenance, he said.

UAE healthcare credibility

Hashemi said the rapid approval process highlighted the UAE’s growing standing in global healthcare and pharmaceuticals.

“Historically, advanced therapies would launch in the US and Europe first, and the region would wait. That is changing,” he said.

He credited UAE regulators with moving “at the speed of evidence rather than the speed of bureaucracy,” arguing that many markets add 12 to 18 months of delay after US or European approvals.

The Emirates Drug Establishment said earlier this month that Foundayo’s approval forms part of a strategy to speed patient access to innovative medicines while strengthening the country’s pharmaceutical ecosystem.

Weight-loss medicine boom

The launch comes as obesity care becomes one of the fastest-growing segments of Gulf healthcare, driven by rising rates of Type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease and fatty liver disease.

Hashemi said oral GLP-1 treatments could expand the market further by lowering the psychological barrier for patients unwilling to use injections.

“Demand will grow, unquestionably,” he said. “But the bigger opportunity is not weight loss alone. It is reducing long-term cardiometabolic risk.”

He warned that providers focused only on prescriptions risk missing the bigger challenge of durable outcomes, including preserving muscle mass, managing rebound weight gain and helping patients transition to long-term metabolic health.

Clinical trial data cited by Eli Lilly showed patients taking the highest Foundayo dose lost an average 12.4 per cent of body weight, or around 27 pounds, compared with placebo.

The company has said the pill can be taken without food or water restrictions, a convenience factor that may support adherence.

With several thousand patients already waiting in the UAE, Foundayo’s arrival may offer an early test of how quickly Gulf healthcare systems can scale the next wave of obesity treatment innovation.
